text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 12 September 2025 

Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Harris, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: H937520 NHS No. 1029384756  
12 Meadow View, Bromley, Kent BR1 2XZ

Thank you for asking me to review your patient. He attended today with his partner and gave a clear account of ongoing events that raise concern for seizures. He works in a manual, safety‑sensitive environment involving heights and machinery, and both he and his employer have noticed episodes at or around workdays that are impacting his attendance and confidence.

He describes a stereotyped spell beginning with a brief sense of internal panic and a rising sensation, followed by several minutes during which he becomes unresponsive to questions, vocalises, and rocks in place according to witnesses. There may be clenched jaw and heavy breathing. He is usually confused afterwards for up to 45–60 minutes with headache and fatigue. He may go five days without seizures, but when they happen he often has them in batches, with 3 - 4 occurring within 24 hours. These clusters tend to occur later in the week, particularly after early shifts or when he has missed sleep. His partner has also witnessed occasional nocturnal episodes of abrupt arousal with gasping and disorientation, after which he has no memory and feels drained the next morning.

He has not yet tolerated several medications tried in the past due to mood changes and dizziness, and is currently taking no regular antiseizure therapy. There is no clear precipitant beyond sleep loss and work‑related stress. He denies alcohol misuse. He has had no recent head injury. There is no history to suggest prolonged convulsive activity, and he has not required emergency treatment in the past year. He reports intermittent low mood, largely related to the unpredictability of the events and concerns about job security. There is no current psychotic symptomatology and no safeguarding concerns were raised.

On examination today he was alert and oriented, with normal speech and language between episodes. Cranial nerves, limb power, coordination and gait were unremarkable. Cardiovascular and general examination were within normal limits.

Given the description and impact on a safety‑critical occupation, further characterisation is required. I have discussed inpatient video‑EEG telemetry to capture typical events and correlate with EEG, alongside MRI brain with an epilepsy protocol if not recently performed. He is agreeable to this plan. In the interim, he and his employer have been advised to continue existing workplace risk assessments and to avoid lone working at height or around moving machinery until we have clearer information from investigations; he understands this and will liaise with occupational health. We also discussed sleep hygiene, regular medication timing for any future treatments, and keeping a seizure diary to document clusters and potential triggers.

I will update you following telemetry and imaging, and we can then consider appropriate therapy and rescue strategies for clusters if indicated. Please let me know if there are additional details from the workplace observations that would help inform the diagnostic work‑up.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter clearly describes clustered seizures: "He may go five days without seizures, but when they happen he often has them in batches, with 3 - 4 occurring within 24 hours." This indicates a pattern of seizure-free intervals up to 5 days, followed by a cluster within a 24-hour period of 3 to 4 seizures. The instruction is to choose the highest frequency; clusters within 24 hours represent the higher-frequency dimension, and the inter-cluster interval is suggested as up to five days without seizures. Therefore, normalize as a cluster frequency with seizures per cluster. Use '5 day' as the inter-cluster period and '3 to 4 per cluster' for seizures within a cluster.
seizure_frequency_number: 1 cluster per 5 day, 3 to 4 per cluster
He may go five days without seizures, but when they happen he often has them in batches, with 3 - 4 occurring within 24 hours.
reference: 1 cluster per 5 day, 3 to 4 per cluster
